Motions are voted on in reverse order of their proposal, with the highest-ranking motion voted on first
All motions are voted on in the order they were proposed, from first to last
The assembly votes on all proposed amendments before voting on any privileged motions
THE MOTIONS SYSTEM.explanation: Motions are voted on in reverse order of their proposal—the motion with the highest rank (closest to the top of the precedence list) is voted on first, and the main motion is always last. This ensures that procedural motions and those meant to modify or dispose of the main motion are decided before the main motion itself.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: The process by which a member formally proposes an action for the assembly to consider is called a(n) ___.
options: second
debate
amendment
motion
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: A motion is the formal proposal made by a member stating 'I move that ...' to bring business before the assembly. A second shows support but is not itself the proposal, debate follows the motion, and an amendment modifies an existing motion rather than proposing new business.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: The formal declaration by the chair that a motion is now before the assembly for debate and voting is known as ___.
options: seconding the motion
putting the question
stating the question
recognizing the member
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Stating the question is when the chair formally announces 'It is moved and seconded that ...' to bring the motion officially before the assembly. Recognizing the member gives them the floor to speak, putting the question means taking the vote, and seconding is the support shown by another member—all distinct from this formal declaration.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: In parliamentary procedure, what is the term for the automatic continuation of business that was not completed at the previous meeting and comes before the assembly without being called for by the chair?
options: Unfinished business and general orders
New business
Special orders
General consent items
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Unfinished business and general orders is the proper parliamentary term for business left over from the previous meeting that automatically returns for consideration. It is distinct from 'new business' (matters not previously raised) and 'special orders' (items specifically set for the current meeting). The source material explicitly notes the term is 'unfinished business,' not the colloquial 'old business.'

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: Which of the following would be required to verify a voice vote result if a member demands verification on a motion to change the organization's bylaws?
options: A ballot vote, since all bylaw changes must use secret ballots
A rising vote, because verification of 2/3 votes requires members to be counted individually
A show of hands vote, since a small group method is faster
A roll call vote, which records each member's name and their position
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Changing bylaws requires a two-thirds majority vote, which by parliamentary rules must be verified by a rising vote (members standing to be counted individually). A voice vote alone (ayes and noes by vocal response) cannot be reliably verified for the precision needed to confirm 2/3 approval. While ballot votes and roll call votes are valid methods in other contexts, a rising vote is the standard verification method when a voice vote result is challenged on a 2/3 question.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: When a motion is brought before the assembly and a member calls for verification because a 2/3 vote is required, what voting method must the chair use?
options: Show of hands, since it allows the chair to see exactly how many members voted on each side
Ballot vote, because all 2/3 votes must be recorded in writing for the minutes
Rising vote, because individual counting of members is necessary to confirm a 2/3 majority
Voice vote, since the chair can simply recount the ayes and noes
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: A 2/3 vote requires members to be counted individually to verify the proportion; a voice vote cannot provide that precision. A rising vote is the standard method to verify any vote and is mandatory when a 2/3 outcome must be confirmed. Ballot and show-of-hands votes serve different purposes and are not required here.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: When a member wishes to participate in debate, the proper procedure begins with the member rising and addressing the chair using which of the following forms?
options: Rising and saying 'Madam President' or 'Mr. President'
Addressing the chair by their first name and stating the topic
Standing and announcing their name followed by 'I have a question'
Raising their hand and waiting to be acknowledged by another member
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: The correct form of addressing the chair is 'Madam President' or 'Mr. President' — this is the formal, recognized way a member gains initial attention before the chair can grant recognition. This step precedes the chair's recognition, which is the second step in the process of bringing a member into debate.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: When determining whether a motion passes by majority vote, all of the following are true EXCEPT
options: A majority is more than half of the votes actually cast by members entitled to vote
Blank votes and abstentions are counted toward the total needed for a majority
Members are not compelled to abstain and may choose not to vote
If 24 votes are cast, a motion needs 13 votes to pass by majority
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Blanks and abstentions are explicitly NOT counted when calculating a majority — only votes actually cast are included in the denominator. A majority requires more than half of votes cast, so abstentions reduce the denominator, not increase the threshold. The other three options correctly state that majority is based on votes cast, that the calculation follows the 'more than half' rule, and that abstention is voluntary.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: During a meeting, the chair has completed debate on a motion and must now determine the outcome by taking a vote. The organization's bylaws require that any change to meeting procedures must be decided by a two-thirds majority. A member demands verification of the vote. What voting method must the chair use to ensure the result can be properly verified?
options: Voice vote, because the chair's word is final on any vote count
Roll call vote, because only named votes can be challenged by members
Ballot vote, to create a written record that can be recounted later
Rising vote, because individual counting of members is necessary to verify a two-thirds majority
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: A rising vote is required when verification is demanded, especially for two-thirds votes, because it allows members to be counted individually and the count to be confirmed or challenged. Voice votes cannot be verified to that level of precision. While ballots and roll calls create records, a rising vote is the standard method RONR specifies for verification and two-thirds tallies.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: In parliamentary procedure, a motion that is reported by a committee to the assembly differs from a motion made from the floor in that a committee-reported motion requires which of the following?
options: A second from another member before the chair states the question
Three members to rise and address the chair in support
A two-thirds vote to be considered by the full assembly
No second — it comes before the assembly directly upon presentation
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Committee-reported motions are an exception to the general six-step process for main motions — they do not require a second because the committee's presentation already demonstrates that more than one member supports considering it. This distinction is crucial for understanding how committees streamline business flow without needing the formal second that motions from the floor require.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: In parliamentary procedure, when a member has been recognized by the chair and is allowed to speak, that member is said to have which of the following?
options: The right to make a motion
Precedence over other business
The floor
The authority to call for a vote
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: When the chair recognizes a member, that member 'has the floor,' meaning they alone may speak at that moment. This is the proper parliamentary term for the right to be heard in debate or to present a motion.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: The process of taking a vote in which the chair asks members to say 'aye' or 'no' aloud, and the chair judges which response is louder, is known as ___.
options: voice vote
rising vote
unanimous consent
show of hands
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: A voice vote, or viva voce, is the normal method for taking a majority vote in parliamentary procedure. The chair listens to the oral responses and determines the result by judging which side was louder, making it quick and efficient for routine decisions.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: Which of the following voting procedures would be appropriate for an organization holding an election of officers, where the bylaws require that votes be kept confidential?
options: Rising vote, because it allows the chair to verify the count by seeing each member
Ballot vote, because confidentiality requires that each member's choice remain secret
Unanimous consent, because it confirms that all members agree on the results
Voice vote, because it is the fastest method and most members prefer efficiency
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Ballot voting is the method used when secrecy is required, such as in elections or whenever bylaws mandate confidential voting. This method ensures that no member's vote is publicly revealed, protecting voter privacy and preventing peer pressure or retaliation.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: In parliamentary procedure, the proper form for a member to propose an action to the assembly is to say which of the following?
options: So move the following measure for consideration
I so move that we take this step forward
I move that the assembly proceed with this action
I make a motion that the assembly adopt this proposal
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: The correct phrasing is 'I move that...' — this is the formal, recognized way to introduce a motion. Phrases like 'I make a motion that,' 'so move,' or 'I so move' are improper forms and will not be recognized in parliamentary procedure.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: A motion brought before the assembly differs in its procedural requirements depending on its source. Committee-reported motions and motions made from the floor are treated differently in that committee-reported motions require which of the following?
options: No second, because they are presented on behalf of the committee
A two-thirds majority instead of a simple majority to pass
Both a second and a formal debate period before any vote
The maker of the motion to speak first before any other member
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Committee-reported motions do not require a second because they are already endorsed by the committee that brings them before the assembly. This distinguishes them from floor motions, which must be seconded to show that at least two members wish to have the question considered.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: In a meeting with 50 members entitled to vote, 32 votes are cast on a main motion with no abstentions or blanks recorded. How many votes would be needed to pass this motion by a two-thirds majority?
options: 17 votes, since two-thirds of the 50 members entitled to vote is approximately 33
22 votes, since two-thirds of the 32 votes cast is approximately 21
24 votes, since two-thirds of 32 is the minimum required to pass under RONR
25 votes, since more than half of the 50 members must approve any motion
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: A two-thirds vote is calculated as two-thirds of the votes CAST, not of all members entitled to vote. With 32 votes cast, two-thirds equals approximately 21.3, which rounds to 22 votes needed. This distinction between votes cast and total membership is critical in parliamentary voting calculations.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: In parliamentary procedure, when a member who has not yet spoken in debate seeks to address the assembly while another member is still delivering their first speech, what must occur?
options: The second member's request is noted, but debate is closed and a vote is taken instead
The chair may interrupt the current speaker to allow the second member to speak
The second member must wait until the current speaker has completed both of their allowed speeches
The second member may speak immediately after the current speaker finishes their first speech
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Parliamentary procedure requires that no member may deliver a second speech on a motion while another member who has not yet spoken seeks the floor. This rule ensures all members get a first opportunity to be heard before anyone speaks twice. The second member's right to speak first takes priority over the current speaker's second speech opportunity.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: In the standard order of business, which of the following correctly describes when special orders are addressed in relation to other agenda items?
options: Special orders are discussed only after all reports have been completed and new business has been concluded
Special orders come before unfinished business, including any postponed items designated as special orders
Special orders are handled after unfinished business and before new business
Special orders must be addressed immediately after the reading and approval of minutes, before any officer reports
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: According to the standard order of business, special orders (which include postponed items made special orders) are placed at item 6, coming after all committee reports but before unfinished business and general orders. This positioning ensures that matters the assembly has designated as urgent receive prompt attention in the meeting sequence.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: In parliamentary procedure, when a member has finished their first speech on a motion and another member who has not yet spoken seeks recognition, what must happen?
options: The member seeking the floor may interrupt to ensure fairness in debate
The member who has already spoken may yield the floor to speak again if they wish
The second speaker must wait until all other members have had their first opportunity to speak
The chair may recognize whoever they prefer, as long as debate remains open
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Parliamentary procedure protects new speakers by requiring that no member speak a second time while another who has not yet spoken seeks the floor. This ensures all members get an equal first opportunity to participate in debate before anyone takes a second turn. This is a core protection of the debate rules to maintain fairness.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: In parliamentary procedure, when a member rises and addresses the chair, the proper form of address is 'Madam President' or 'Mr. President.' What is the correct term for what the member is doing in this opening step?
options: Obtaining recognition
Seeking permission to speak
Calling for the floor
Requesting the chair's attention
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: While all options describe related actions, 'obtaining recognition' is the precise parliamentary term for the formal process by which a member addresses the chair and prepares to be recognized. Recognition is the chair's response; the member's action of rising and addressing the chair is the first step toward obtaining it.

RUNNING BUSINESS.question: The process of allowing members to express their views and concerns about a pending motion before a vote is taken makes up ___.
options: Discourse
Voting
Deliberation
Debate
RUNNING BUSINESS.explanation: Debate is the formal parliamentary term for the period during which recognized members speak for or against a motion. While deliberation and discourse are related concepts, debate is the specific parliamentary procedure that comes after the question is stated and before the vote is taken.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: The official record of what actions were taken during a meeting, including motions made and their results, is called the ___.
options: order of business
bylaws
minutes
agenda
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: Minutes are the permanent written record of what was done at a meeting—the motions made, who made them, and the results—not a summary of debate or discussion. This is a core secretary responsibility and distinguishes minutes from other meeting documents like agendas or bylaws.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: Which of the following would ALL be included in the official minutes of a regular meeting?
options: The treasurer's approved budget, amendments to the bylaws, and a list of all guests in attendance
The kind of meeting, date and place, all main motions with the maker's name, and the result of each motion
The names of all members who spoke, verbatim quotes from the discussion, and opinions expressed by board members
A summary of the debate on each motion, member attendance records, and the chair's personal observations
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: Minutes must record what was done—the type of meeting, date, place, main motions with the maker named, and results—but must NOT include debate summaries, opinions, or praise/criticism. The source material is explicit about what belongs and what does not.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: The role of the parliamentarian differs from that of the chair in that the parliamentarian ___.
options: advises the chair on procedure but has no authority to make rulings independently
makes final rulings on points of order and enforces all rules of procedure
signs all official documents and certifies the accuracy of records
presides over the meeting and announces business in proper order
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: The parliamentarian advises the chair on rules but does not rule; only the chair has the authority to decide points of order (subject to appeal). The parliamentarian also remains impartial and does not make motions, debate, or vote except by ballot, making it a purely advisory role.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: The treasurer's report at a meeting is handled differently from the auditor's report. The treasurer's report is ___.
options: approved by the membership as a whole
reviewed by the finance committee before being presented
adopted by majority vote after discussion
received and filed without formal adoption
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: The treasurer's report is received and filed — it is NOT adopted or approved like other reports. Only the auditor's report receives formal adoption. This distinction is a key procedural rule that distinguishes the two officer reports.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: A committee created to handle a specific fundraising project and expected to disband once the project is complete is classified as a ___.
options: special committee
standing committee
quasi-committee
committee of the whole
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: A special (also called select or ad hoc) committee is created for a single task and ceases to exist once that task is completed and the final report is made. A standing committee, by contrast, is permanent and defined in the bylaws.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: A motion to close nominations removes members' right to nominate further candidates and therefore requires what fraction of the vote to pass?
options: Three-fourths of the membership
A simple majority
Two-thirds of the votes cast
Unanimous consent
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: Closing nominations is a rights-removing motion and therefore requires a two-thirds vote. The source material explicitly states that closing nominations requires 2/3 because it restricts the membership's procedural rights.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: During a meeting, a member nominates a candidate for treasurer from the floor. A second member then immediately says, 'I second that nomination.' Which of the following correctly describes what just happened?
options: The nominee is now officially a candidate and the second was proper parliamentary procedure
The nomination was valid, but the second was unnecessary because nominations from the floor require no second
The nomination was improper because floor nominations must first be approved by the nominating committee
The second invalidated the nomination because only one person per floor nomination is allowed to speak
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: Nominations from the floor are a unique motion type that require NO second. While the member's attempt to second was well-intentioned, it was not required and does not affect the validity of the nomination. The source material explicitly states that nominations from the floor require no second, making this a point of procedural distinction that many members confuse.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: In the absence of both the president and vice president, who has the responsibility to call the meeting to order and preside over the election of a chair pro tem?
options: Any member of the assembly, selected by majority vote before the meeting begins
The treasurer, as the next-ranking officer in the organization's hierarchy
The parliamentarian, who maintains procedural knowledge and impartiality
The secretary, based on their role as keeper of official records and procedures
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: The secretary is designated to call the meeting to order and preside over the election of a chair pro tem when both the president and vice president are absent. This power flows from the secretary's core responsibility as custodian of official records and procedures, making them the natural procedural authority in this emergency situation.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: Which of the following would ALL result in a motion being ruled out of order by the chair?
options: A motion to debate a topic already covered in the previous meeting's minutes, a motion made during a recess, and a motion that is shorter than five words
A motion made by a member with fewer than two years of membership, a motion seconded by the chair, and a motion that requires a voice vote instead of a show of hands
A motion proposed while another member holds the floor, a motion that conflicts with an existing adopted motion, and a motion that restates a question already decided earlier this session
A motion made after the agenda has been approved, a motion that includes the word 'therefore,' and a motion that is spoken rather than written on the record
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: Motions that conflict with bylaws or with an existing adopted motion still in force, motions proposed while another member has the floor, and motions that restate a substantially identical question already decided in the current session are all inherently out of order. The correct option lists three distinct categories from the source material's improper-motions list. The other options include criteria (membership duration, vote method, word count, timing relative to recess or agenda) that do not automatically render motions out of order.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: When a special committee completes its assigned task and submits its final report to the assembly, what happens to that committee?
options: It transitions to standing-committee status and becomes a permanent fixture in the bylaws.
It automatically merges with the standing finance committee to avoid duplication.
It remains active but enters a dormant state until the assembly votes to reactivate it.
It ceases to exist, having fulfilled its one-time purpose.
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: A special (or ad hoc) committee is created for a single, specific task and is dissolved once that task is complete and the final report is made. This distinguishes it from a standing committee, which is permanent and defined in the bylaws. The source material explicitly states that special committees 'cease to exist when the task is done and the final report is made.'

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: The process by which a parliamentary organization determines which candidate has received enough votes to fill an elected office is called
options: ballot certification
majority of ballots cast
election finalization
plurality voting
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: Under parliamentary procedure, a majority of ballots cast elects a candidate, unless the bylaws provide for plurality instead. This is the standard threshold; if no candidate achieves it on the first ballot, balloting is repeated until someone does. The term 'majority of ballots cast' precisely describes this core electoral principle.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: During an election, one ballot contains a vote for a candidate who is not eligible for the office, and another ballot is left blank. How are these votes counted when determining whether a candidate has achieved the majority needed to be elected?
options: The invalid vote counts as a vote cast, but the blank does not; majority is calculated from all votes cast
The invalid vote is discarded, but the blank counts as a vote cast for the candidate receiving the most other votes
Both the invalid vote and the blank are excluded; only legitimate votes are used to calculate the majority
Both count equally as votes cast and reduce the majority threshold accordingly
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: According to parliamentary procedure rules, illegal votes (those cast for ineligible persons) are counted as votes CAST for purposes of computing the majority, while blanks are not counted. This means the invalid vote contributes to the total ballot count but does not help any candidate achieve the needed majority, whereas a blank is simply excluded from the total.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: Which of the following would ALL be duties of the vice president in a parliamentary organization?
options: Presiding over meetings in the president's absence and automatically assuming the presidency if a vacancy occurs
Advising the chair on parliamentary procedure and remaining impartial during debate
Serving as custodian of the organization's funds and reporting regularly on financial matters
Keeping official records, maintaining the membership roll, and providing the president with the order of business
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: The vice president's core duties are to preside when the president is absent and to automatically fill a presidential vacancy unless bylaws specify otherwise. The other options describe the secretary's, treasurer's, and parliamentarian's roles respectively. This tests understanding of which specific responsibilities belong to the vice president role.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: Which of the following would ALL be proper duties of the president during a meeting?
options: Advising the chair on points of procedure, deciding points of order without appeal, and voting on all motions except by ballot
Serving as custodian of organization funds, paying authorized bills, and presenting a financial report for adoption
Keeping the official minutes, maintaining the membership roll, and certifying copies of official documents
Recognizing members who wish to speak, announcing business in proper order, and enforcing the rules of parliamentary procedure
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: The president (chair) opens meetings on time, announces business in proper order, recognizes members, enforces rules, and decides points of order. The other options describe duties belonging to the secretary (B), treasurer (C), and incorrectly describe the parliamentarian's role (D) — the parliamentarian advises only and has no independent authority to rule.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: The procedures and practices by which a parliamentary organization identifies and appoints or elects individuals to leadership roles, following rules about nominations, seconding requirements, and voting thresholds, make up the ___.
options: Nomination process
Election and nomination process
Officer selection protocol
Committee assignment structure
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: The election and nomination process encompasses the full system of how candidates are proposed, nominated (with or without seconds depending on method), and voted on to fill elected offices. While nomination process (A) captures part of it, the complete system includes both nomination and election mechanics. Committee assignment (C) and officer selection protocol (D) are not the formal parliamentary term for this procedure.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: Which of the following would ALL be proper ways to nominate a candidate for office in a parliamentary organization?
options: From the floor only, by the chair only, or by the parliamentarian
By majority vote, by ballot, or by unanimous consent
By the chair, from the floor, or by mail
By a nominating committee, by petition, or by write-in ballot
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: The source material lists nomination methods as: by the chair, from the floor (most common), by a nominating committee, by ballot, by mail, and by petition. Option B correctly identifies three of these valid methods. Options A, C, and D mix in invalid or non-existent nomination procedures.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: Which of the following would ALL be proper responsibilities of the treasurer in a parliamentary organization?
options: Serve as custodian of funds, pay authorized bills, and report regularly to the membership
Review the annual budget, audit the financial records, and certify that all bills are legitimate
Approve all expenditures before they are made and sign all checks issued by the organization
Maintain the membership roll, authorize purchases, and file financial reports with state authorities
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: The treasurer's core duties are to safeguard the organization's money, authorize and pay bills that have been properly approved by the assembly, and keep the membership informed through regular financial reports. The other options conflate the treasurer's role with those of other officers (secretary, auditor) or assign authority the treasurer does not have.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: Which of the following would ALL result in a motion being properly ruled out of order?
options: A member raises a point of order during the motion's discussion; the motion requires a two-thirds vote to pass; the motion is made while the assembly is considering a related topic
The motion is debated for more than ten minutes; the motion is proposed by a member who has already made three motions today; the chair decides the motion is unnecessary

The motion involves spending money without the treasurer's advance approval; the motion is made by a member not serving on the relevant committee; the motion would require a special meeting to implement
A member makes the motion while another member has the floor; the motion contradicts a rule in the bylaws; the motion proposes the same action already voted on and defeated in the previous meeting
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: A motion is always out of order if it is proposed while another member holds the floor (violates floor control), if it conflicts with the bylaws or law (violates organizational rules), or if it presents substantially the same question already decided in the current session (prevents repetitive voting). The correct answer identifies three genuine procedural barriers; the other options conflate unrelated factors (debate length, cost approval, committee membership) with the actual rules that render a motion improper.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: The process by which a special committee reports its findings to the assembly, including the right of committee members who disagree with the majority view to present their own perspective, is governed by rules about committee reports and ___.
options: ordered referral
appeal procedures
minority reports
reconsideration motions
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: While a committee's majority report is presented formally, parliamentary procedure specifically allows a minority of the committee to submit a minority report — a written statement of their differing views — but only with the assembly's permission. This is the formal mechanism that protects dissenting committee members' right to be heard.

OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.question: Which of the following would ALL be included in the minutes of a membership meeting?
options: A summary of the debate on each motion; the names of members who spoke in favor or against proposals; a record of the chair's personal opinions on the business conducted
The type of meeting, the date and location, whether the president and secretary were present, and the time the meeting ended
A detailed transcript of all discussion; the number of members present; recommendations made by individual members
The reasons members gave for voting a certain way; the chair's interpretation of parliamentary rules applied; the agenda items that were skipped
OFFICERS, RECORDS, COMMITTEES, ELECTIONS.explanation: Minutes record ACTIONS taken and facts about the meeting itself — not debate or opinions. The correct option captures the essential administrative details: meeting type, date, location, officer presence, and adjournment time. Options A, C, and D all include debate summaries, personal views, or interpretive commentary, which do not belong in an objective record of actions.

FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.question: The structure and governance system that includes nine national officers, five regional divisions, and a Board of Directors managing business strategy and program implementation makes up the ___.
options: FBLA national organizational structure
NAP partnership system
local chapter organization
state chapter framework

FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.question: A motion to amend is pending. Which of the following motions would be in order at this time?
options: A motion to postpone indefinitely
A motion to adjourn
A motion to limit debate
A motion to reconsider a previous vote
FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.explanation: When a motion to amend is pending, a motion to limit debate (which requires a two-thirds vote) is in order because it is a higher-ranked subsidiary motion that can apply to the pending question. A motion to postpone indefinitely, reconsider, or adjourn would either rank lower than the amendment or be out of order in this context, as they do not properly address the pending amendment.

FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.question: The motion to suspend the rules is best described as a procedural action that ___.
options: requires a simple majority vote and can be used at any time during a meeting
allows a member to prevent discussion of a motion without voting
extends the time limit for debate on any pending motion
temporarily sets aside parliamentary procedure to accomplish a specific urgent purpose
FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.explanation: Suspend the rules is a two-thirds motion that allows an assembly to temporarily abandon its normal parliamentary rules for a specific, stated purpose—such as taking up business out of order or expediting a process. It is not the same as limiting debate (which restricts time) or preventing discussion altogether (which is objection to consideration). The two-thirds requirement and the requirement to state the purpose are what distinguish it from ordinary procedural motions.

FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.question: A motion to object to consideration is pending. Which of the following motions would be in order?
options: A motion to limit debate on the main motion
A motion to appeal the chair's ruling on the objection
A motion to reconsider the main motion
A motion to amend the main motion
FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.explanation: When an objection to consideration is raised, an appeal of the chair's ruling on that objection is in order—this allows the assembly to overrule the chair's decision. Motions to reconsider, amend, or limit debate on the main motion itself are not in order until the objection is resolved, because the whole point of the objection is to prevent consideration of the main motion in the first place.

The state charter (which requires at least five active local chapters for HS division), timely national dues payment, and chapter renewal all directly affect NLC eligibility.

a motion that can be reconsidered by any member at any time

options: A motion to reconsider a previous vote
A motion to amend the main motion
A motion to lay on the table
A motion to call for the orders of the day
FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.explanation: When a motion to postpone indefinitely is pending, a motion to lay on the table is in order because it takes precedence over the postpone indefinitely motion. Lay on the table ranks higher in precedence and can interrupt the pending motion. A motion to amend the main motion would also be pending and cannot be made when another motion is already on the floor seeking to dispose of the main question.

FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.question: The motion to limit debate is best described as a procedural action that ___.
options: allows the chair to decide unilaterally how much time is spent on discussion
suspends all parliamentary rules for the remainder of the meeting
requires a simple majority vote and restricts the number of times members may speak
permanently ends the current debate and moves directly to a final vote
FBLA BYLAWS AND ORGANIZATION (the ~10 by.explanation: The motion to limit debate requires a two-thirds vote and allows the assembly to restrict how many times or for how long members may speak on a pending motion. It is a legitimate way to manage discussion time while preserving the right to debate, unlike motions that completely suspend procedure or give unilateral chair power. This motion is classified as incidental and requires a two-thirds majority.
